title: Authorized Access Control
controlText: Limit information system access to authorized users, processes acting on behalf of authorized users, or devices (including other information systems).
plainEnglish: Only people, automated processes, and devices that you have explicitly approved should be able to log into or connect to your systems. You need to know who and what is authorized, document it, and have technical controls that enforce that list. This is the foundation that everything else in Access Control builds on.

objectives.text: authorized users are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: A current, maintained list of named users with access to in-scope systems, with roles documented. New hires get added through a defined onboarding process; departures get removed promptly. The list should match what is actually in the identity provider.
commonEvidence: Identity provider user export (Entra ID, Okta, Active Directory)

objectives.text: processes acting on behalf of authorized users are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: An inventory of service accounts, scheduled tasks, scripts, RPA bots, and CI/CD pipelines, each tied to a named human owner and a documented business purpose. If nobody can say what a service account does, that is a finding.
commonEvidence: Service account inventory with owner and purpose
Scheduled task and cron job list

objectives.text: devices (and other systems) authorized to connect to the system are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: An asset inventory covering endpoints, servers, network gear, printers, IoT/OT, and any external systems with connectivity. Should include hostname, MAC, owner, location, and classification. External system connections should have written agreements.
commonEvidence: Interconnection Security Agreements (ISAs) for external systems

objectives.text: system access is limited to authorized users
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement preventing unlisted users from logging in. Default-deny posture on join. Periodic access reviews that actually catch and remove stale accounts. Evidence that disabled accounts cannot authenticate.
commonEvidence: Identity provider login logs showing denied attempts
Quarterly user access review records with sign-off
Conditional access or login policy screenshots

objectives.text: system access is limited to processes acting on behalf of authorized users
objectives.whatToLookFor: Service accounts cannot be used outside their intended purpose. API keys are scoped. Automation runs with least privilege. No 'god mode' service accounts shared across unrelated systems.
commonEvidence: API key inventory with scopes and expiration

objectives.text: system access is limited to authorized devices (including other systems)
objectives.whatToLookFor: Network access control (NAC), 802.1X, or device compliance policies block unknown devices. Conditional access requires enrolled and compliant devices. System-to-system traffic restricted at the firewall to known endpoints.

commonArtifacts: User account roster or access matrix
Identity provider user and group exports
commonGaps: Personal devices connecting to corporate resources without enrollment
Service accounts with no documented owner or purpose
Stale accounts from departed employees still active
No mechanism to identify or block unknown devices on the network
'Authorized users' list living only in someone's head, not documented
Shared admin credentials with no individual accountability
Vendor or contractor accounts still active long after engagement ends
Termination process exists on paper but is not consistently executed

title: Transaction & Function Control
controlText: Limit information system access to the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute.
plainEnglish: It is not enough to say 'user X can log in.' You need to control what they can do once they are in. Define each role's permitted actions (read, write, update, delete, configure) and enforce that with technical controls — usually role-based access control. Privileged functions should be separated from everyday user functions.

objectives.text: the types of transactions and functions that authorized users are permitted to execute are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented role definitions tied to business functions. Each role lists the applications, modules, and CRUD-style operations it can perform. Privileged versus non-privileged roles are distinguished. The matrix is current — not a stale spreadsheet from two years ago.
commonEvidence: RBAC role definitions in identity provider
Access Control Policy section addressing role-based access

objectives.text: system access is limited to the defined types of transactions and functions for authorized users
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement actually matches the documented matrix. Group memberships line up with assigned roles. No users with permissions exceeding their documented role. Spot-check a few users and confirm their actual access matches what the matrix says it should be.
commonEvidence: Group membership exports compared against role matrix
Access review evidence reconciling actual versus intended permissions

commonArtifacts: RBAC configuration in identity provider and applications
Access review logs with reconciliation evidence
commonGaps: Excessive group memberships granting more access than the role requires
No documented role-permission matrix — assignments made ad hoc
Domain admin or application admin sprawl
'Temporary' elevated access never revoked
Job changes not triggering permission updates (permission accumulation)
Custom application roles undocumented
Read-only roles given write access 'just in case'
Matrix exists but does not reflect what is actually configured in the system

title: Control CUI Flow
controlText: Control the flow of CUI in accordance with approved authorizations.
plainEnglish: This is about where CUI is allowed to travel — between networks, segments, systems, and external parties — independent of who can read it. You need policies that define which sources may send CUI to which destinations, and technical controls (firewalls, proxies, DLP, encryption, secure file transfer) that actually enforce those flows. This is one of the controls assessors dig into hardest because it ties directly to your data flow diagram.

objectives.text: information flow control policies are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: A written information flow policy that addresses CUI specifically — not generic 'data protection' language. Should cover email, file transfer, internet egress, internal segmentation, and partner/subcontractor transfers. Must be approved and current.

objectives.text: methods and enforcement mechanisms for controlling the flow of CUI are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented technical mechanisms — firewall rule sets, proxy configs, DLP rules, encryption requirements, approved secure file transfer tools — each tied to a specific flow scenario in the policy.
commonEvidence: Firewall rule documentation with stated purpose per rule
Web proxy or secure web gateway configuration
Approved secure file transfer tool documentation (DoD SAFE, Kiteworks, encrypted email gateway)

objectives.text: designated sources and destinations (e.g., networks, individuals, and devices) for CUI within the system and between interconnected systems are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: A current data flow diagram showing where CUI originates, where it is stored, and every authorized destination — including prime contractor portals, government customer systems, and internal enclaves. Interconnection agreements exist for external destinations.

objectives.text: authorizations for controlling the flow of CUI are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Explicit allow/deny authorizations: who can send CUI to whom, under what conditions (encrypted, approved channel, manager approval). Tied back to roles and contractual requirements.
commonEvidence: Allow/deny rule sets with documented authorization
Approval records for partner or subcontractor flows

objectives.text: approved authorizations for controlling the flow of CUI are enforced
objectives.whatToLookFor: Live technical enforcement — DLP blocks unauthorized sends, firewall denies are logged, encrypted channels are mandatory not optional. There is evidence that violations are detected, investigated, and resolved.
commonEvidence: Firewall deny logs for CUI-relevant rules
Exception or violation tickets with resolution

commonArtifacts: Firewall rule set with documented purpose per rule
DLP policy configuration and alert evidence
commonGaps: No data flow diagram showing where CUI actually travels
Flat network with no segmentation between CUI and non-CUI environments
CUI sent over standard email without encryption
No DLP — flows controlled only by user judgment and training
Allow-all firewall egress rules
Subcontractor/partner transfers happen ad hoc through personal email or file shares
Cloud-to-cloud CUI flows undocumented (e.g., M365 to a partner tenant)
No mechanism to detect when policy is violated — assumed compliance
Policy says 'use encrypted channels' but no list of approved channels

title: Separation of Duties
controlText: Separate the duties of individuals to reduce the risk of malevolent activity without collusion.
plainEnglish: No single person should be able to complete a sensitive end-to-end process alone. Identify the duty pairs that create conflict-of-interest or 'too much power' risks (typically: requesting vs approving access, developing vs deploying code, administering systems vs reviewing audit logs) and assign them to different people. Technical enforcement matters — splitting duties on paper while one admin still holds every credential is not separation.

objectives.text: the duties of individuals requiring separation are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented list of duty pairs that must be separated, with rationale. Common pairings: account provisioning vs approval, code commit vs production deploy, payment initiation vs approval, system admin vs audit log review.
commonEvidence: Access Control Policy section addressing SoD
Documented justification for each defined separation

objectives.text: responsibilities for duties that require separation are assigned to separate individuals
objectives.whatToLookFor: Role assignments actually split the duties across different named people. Job descriptions or role documentation reflects the split. No 'backup' arrangements that quietly defeat the separation.
commonEvidence: Org chart with security roles mapped

objectives.text: access privileges that enable individuals to exercise the duties that require separation are granted to separate individuals
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical permissions enforce the split. The person who can request an account cannot also approve it. The developer who commits code cannot also deploy to production. Group membership audits confirm no individual holds both halves of a separated pair.
commonEvidence: Group membership audit comparing assignments to SoD matrix
Workflow tool configuration showing separation enforcement (e.g., approval workflows)
CI/CD pipeline permissions showing developer vs deployer split

commonArtifacts: Group membership audit comparing actual assignments to SoD matrix
commonGaps: Single IT admin holds every privileged credential — no separation possible
SoD documented but bypassed via 'emergency access' shared accounts
Audit log reviewers are the same admins generating the logs
Small organizations claim SoD is impractical without compensating controls (manager review, periodic third-party audit)
Developers can deploy directly to production

title: Least Privilege
controlText: Employ the principle of least privilege, including for specific security functions and privileged accounts.
plainEnglish: Every user, process, and account should have only the permissions needed to do its job — no more. This applies especially to privileged accounts (domain admin, root, application admin) and security functions (changing access controls, modifying audit settings, managing keys). Permission accumulation over time as people change roles is the most common way least privilege quietly degrades.

objectives.text: privileged accounts are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: An inventory of every privileged account — domain admin, local admin, application admin, root, database sa, cloud root accounts, break-glass accounts. Each entry has a documented owner and purpose.
commonEvidence: Privileged Access Management (PAM) tool roster (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea)
Domain admin and equivalent group membership exports

objectives.text: access to privileged accounts is authorized in accordance with the principle of least privilege
objectives.whatToLookFor: Privileged access is granted only when business need exists, time-limited where possible, and reviewed periodically. Just-in-time elevation rather than standing privilege where the toolset allows.
commonEvidence: Privileged access request and approval records
JIT elevation logs from PAM solution

objectives.text: security functions are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of what counts as a 'security function' in the environment — managing access controls, modifying audit settings, managing cryptographic keys, configuring firewall/IDS, etc.

objectives.text: access to security functions is authorized in accordance with the principle of least privilege
objectives.whatToLookFor: Only specifically authorized personnel can perform security functions. Access grants tie back to documented role need. Spot-check: who can modify firewall rules, who can change audit settings, who can manage cryptographic keys?
commonEvidence: Role assignments for security tool admins
Firewall and security appliance admin user lists

commonArtifacts: Security function inventory and access matrix
commonGaps: Standing domain admin rights instead of just-in-time elevation
No periodic privileged access review — once granted, access persists indefinitely
Cloud root/owner accounts used for daily operations
Permission accumulation from internal job transfers never cleaned up
Privileged accounts not protected by PAM or vaulting
Same admin account used for both day-to-day work and privileged operations

title: Non-Privileged Account Use
controlText: Use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing nonsecurity functions.
plainEnglish: Administrators should have two accounts: a regular user account for everyday work (email, web browsing, document editing) and a separate privileged account used only when administrative work is needed. The principle is to limit blast radius — if an admin's daily-use account is compromised by a phishing email, the attacker should not inherit domain admin rights along with it.

objectives.text: nonsecurity functions are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented distinction between security/administrative tasks and routine business tasks. The list does not need to be exhaustive but should make clear what falls in each category.
commonEvidence: Documented privileged vs non-privileged function definitions
Administrator handbook or runbook addressing account usage

objectives.text: users are required to use non-privileged accounts or roles when accessing nonsecurity functions
objectives.whatToLookFor: Policy requires admins to use their regular account for non-administrative work. Technical enforcement where possible — admin accounts blocked from email and internet, separate workstations for admin tasks, or PAW (Privileged Access Workstation) deployment.
commonEvidence: Policy requiring separate admin and user accounts
Configuration blocking admin accounts from email/web
Audit logs showing privileged account usage patterns

commonArtifacts: Account usage policy distinguishing admin vs daily-use accounts
Email and web filtering rules excluding admin accounts
commonGaps: Admins use a single account for both daily work and privileged actions
Domain admin account checks email and browses the web
No PAW or equivalent — admin work happens from the same workstation as everything else
Policy exists but is not enforced — assumed compliance

title: Privileged Functions
controlText: Prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions and capture the execution of such functions in audit logs.
plainEnglish: Two requirements bundled together: (1) regular users physically cannot execute privileged functions like creating accounts, modifying audit settings, or installing system software; and (2) every time a privileged function does run, it gets logged. The logging side is critical — it's what lets you detect privilege misuse, whether intentional or compromised credentials.

objectives.text: privileged functions are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented list of what counts as a privileged function in the environment. Typical entries: account management, system patching, security configuration changes, cryptographic key management, audit log management, IDS/AV bypass.
commonEvidence: System administration runbook listing privileged operations

objectives.text: non-privileged users are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Clear delineation of who is privileged versus non-privileged. Usually expressed via group membership or role assignments in the IDP.
commonEvidence: Privileged user roster (and by exclusion, non-privileged user definition)

objectives.text: non-privileged users are prevented from executing privileged functions
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical controls actually block non-privileged users from privileged operations. UAC enforced on workstations, sudo restricted on Linux, RBAC enforced in cloud platforms. Spot-check by attempting a privileged operation as a regular user.
commonEvidence: Failed privileged action attempts in audit logs

objectives.text: the execution of privileged functions is captured in audit logs
objectives.whatToLookFor: Privileged actions generate audit records that include who, what, when, and outcome. Logs are protected from tampering. Endpoint detection or PAM solution captures privileged session activity.
commonEvidence: Audit log samples showing privileged function execution
PAM session recordings or command logs

commonArtifacts: EDR or SIEM rule configuration for privileged events
UAC, sudo, and RBAC configuration documentation
Sample audit logs of privileged function execution
commonGaps: Privileged actions logged inconsistently across platforms (Windows captured but Linux not, or vice versa)
Logs exist but no review process to detect anomalies
Non-privileged users have local admin rights on their own workstations 'for convenience'
PowerShell/sudo usage not logged or logged without command detail
Cloud control plane actions not forwarded to centralized logging

title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts
controlText: Limit unsuccessful logon attempts.
plainEnglish: After a defined number of failed login attempts, the account locks. This protects against brute-force password guessing and indicates suspicious activity. The threshold and lockout duration are organization-defined — fewer attempts is more secure but creates more support tickets, so set it deliberately based on risk.

objectives.text: the means of limiting unsuccessful logon attempts is defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented threshold (typical: 3–10 attempts) and lockout duration (typical: 15–30 minutes auto-unlock, or admin unlock required). Applied consistently across in-scope systems including OS, applications, and remote access.
commonEvidence: System security plan section addressing logon attempts

objectives.text: the defined means of limiting unsuccessful logon attempts is implemented
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement of the policy. Verified across the IDP, in-scope applications, and remote access endpoints (VPN, RDP gateway). Test by attempting failed logins and confirming lockout actually occurs.
commonEvidence: Group Policy Object screenshots showing lockout settings
Account lockout events in audit logs
Help desk tickets showing lockouts being processed

commonArtifacts: Help desk runbook for account unlock procedure
Audit log evidence of lockout events
commonGaps: Lockout configured in AD but not in cloud applications or VPN
Threshold set so high (e.g., 50 attempts) that it provides no real protection
Lockout duration of 'permanent until admin unlock' creates DoS risk
No alerting on patterns of lockouts that could indicate brute-force attempts

title: Privacy & Security Notices
controlText: Provide privacy and security notices consistent with applicable CUI rules.
plainEnglish: Display warning banners on systems handling CUI before login. The banner notifies users that the system contains CUI, that use is monitored and recorded, that unauthorized access is prohibited, and that use constitutes consent to monitoring. This is both a legal-protection mechanism (supporting prosecution of unauthorized access) and a deterrent. Get General Counsel review on banner content.

objectives.text: privacy and security notices required by CUI-specified rules are identified, consistent, and associated with the specific CUI category
objectives.whatToLookFor: Banner text drafted (typically with Legal review) that addresses monitoring, prohibition of unauthorized use, criminal/civil penalty notice, consent to monitoring, and CUI-specific requirements such as Export Control. Banner content reflects the actual CUI categories present.
commonEvidence: Banner text document with Legal/General Counsel approval
Mapping of banner language to CUI category requirements

objectives.text: privacy and security notices are displayed
objectives.whatToLookFor: Banner appears before login on every in-scope system — Windows logon, Linux SSH, web applications, VPN clients. Where users access via API or other non-interactive paths, an equivalent notification mechanism is in place. Posted notices for paper-based CUI handling areas.
commonEvidence: Screenshots of login banners across system types
Group Policy or MDM configuration deploying the banner
Photos of physical posted notices in CUI handling areas

commonArtifacts: Approved banner text with Legal review evidence
Screenshots from each in-scope system type
Physical posted notices for CUI-handling areas
commonGaps: Banner deployed to Windows endpoints but not to Linux servers, network gear, or web apps
Generic 'authorized use only' banner without CUI-specific language
No Legal review of banner text
Cloud applications missing banners entirely
Banner text not updated when CUI categories change

title: Session Lock
controlText: Use session lock with pattern-hiding displays to prevent access and viewing of data after a period of inactivity.
plainEnglish: After a defined period of inactivity, the system locks and hides whatever was on the screen behind a screensaver or lock screen. This protects against shoulder-surfing and unauthorized use of unattended workstations. Session lock is a temporary measure — it is not a substitute for fully logging out.

objectives.text: the period of inactivity after which the system initiates a session lock is defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented inactivity timeout (commonly 10–15 minutes) applied across endpoints. Defined in policy and reflected in technical configuration.
commonEvidence: Session lock policy with defined timeout
GPO or MDM screen lock configuration

objectives.text: access to the system and viewing of data is prevented by initiating a session lock after the defined period of inactivity
objectives.whatToLookFor: Lock actually triggers at the defined timeout across all in-scope endpoints. User must re-authenticate to unlock. Test by leaving a workstation idle and observing.
commonEvidence: MDM compliance reports showing lock policy applied

objectives.text: previously visible information is concealed via a pattern-hiding display after the defined period of inactivity
objectives.whatToLookFor: Lock screen hides the previous content — no peeking around the lock dialog. Screensaver does not display CUI (e.g., no document preview screensavers in CUI environments).
commonEvidence: Screenshot of locked screen showing concealed content

commonArtifacts: Endpoint baseline configuration with screen lock settings
Visual confirmation of pattern-hiding lock screen
commonGaps: Inactivity timeout configured too long (60+ minutes) to be meaningful
Linux or macOS endpoints missing the policy applied to Windows
Conference room or shared workstations exempted with no compensating control
Screensaver displays document previews or sensitive data

title: Session Termination
controlText: Terminate (automatically) a user session after a defined condition.
plainEnglish: Different from session lock: this is full session termination — the user is logged out and must reauthenticate completely. Conditions that trigger termination include extended inactivity (longer than the lock threshold), end of business day, detected policy violations, or scheduled maintenance windows. The point is to ensure no session lingers indefinitely on an unattended system.

objectives.text: conditions requiring a user session to terminate are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented termination conditions — typically extended inactivity (e.g., 60+ minutes), end of workday, or specific anomalies. The conditions are organization-defined and should reflect operational reality.
commonEvidence: System security plan section on session management

objectives.text: a user session is automatically terminated after any of the defined conditions occur
objectives.whatToLookFor: Termination actually fires when conditions are met. Verified through testing or audit logs. Note that termination ends user processes (with exceptions for those explicitly created to persist) — not just disconnects from the network.
commonEvidence: Application or VPN session timeout configuration

commonArtifacts: Cloud workspace or VDI session policies
Audit log evidence of automatic terminations
commonGaps: Session termination conflated with session lock — only lock is configured
Web applications and cloud SaaS missing session timeouts
VPN sessions persist for days because of long timeout settings
No termination on workday end for systems with predictable usage windows

title: Control Remote Access
controlText: Monitor and control remote access sessions.
plainEnglish: Any access from outside the trusted network — VPN, jump servers, cloud admin portals, even authorized cloud SaaS — counts as remote access and must be monitored and controlled. This means defined types of allowed remote access, technical enforcement (typically VPN with device posture checks), and active monitoring of sessions. Cloud-based email and file storage are remote access if they contain CUI.

objectives.text: remote access sessions are permitted
objectives.whatToLookFor: Policy explicitly addresses remote access — what is allowed, who is allowed, from what devices, and under what conditions. Default-deny posture rather than default-allow.
commonEvidence: Acceptable use language addressing remote work

objectives.text: the types of permitted remote access are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented inventory of allowed remote access methods — VPN, RDP gateway, SSH bastion, cloud admin portal, browser-based applications. Each method has a stated business purpose and access scope.
commonEvidence: Architecture diagram showing remote access paths

objectives.text: remote access sessions are controlled
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical controls limit remote access to documented methods. Conditional access policies, device compliance, MFA requirements, geo-restrictions where applicable. Unauthorized methods (e.g., personal RDP, third-party remote tools) are blocked.
commonEvidence: VPN client configuration and device requirements
Conditional access policies for cloud applications
Firewall rules blocking unauthorized remote access tools

objectives.text: remote access sessions are monitored
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active monitoring of remote sessions — connection logs reviewed, alerts on anomalies (impossible travel, unusual times, unusual data volumes). Logging captures who connected, when, from where, for how long.
commonEvidence: Alerting rules for anomalous remote access patterns
Periodic review of remote access activity

commonArtifacts: SIEM rules for remote access monitoring
commonGaps: Cloud applications containing CUI not treated as remote access — no monitoring or control
VPN logs collected but never reviewed
Personal remote access tools (TeamViewer, AnyDesk) used without authorization
No device posture check before granting remote access — any device with credentials gets in
Subcontractor or vendor remote access provisioned ad hoc with no documentation

title: Remote Access Confidentiality
controlText: Employ cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions.
plainEnglish: Remote access sessions must be encrypted using FIPS-validated cryptography — not just any encryption, but cryptographic modules that have been formally tested under FIPS 140-2 (or 140-3). This is a frequent finding because using an approved algorithm is not enough; the implementation itself must be validated. VPN products, TLS implementations, and remote access tools all need to be checked against the NIST CMVP list.

objectives.text: cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identification of cryptographic mechanisms in use — VPN protocols and FIPS validation status, TLS configurations, SSH cipher suites. Each ties to a specific FIPS-validated module on the CMVP list.
commonEvidence: List of remote access cryptographic mechanisms

objectives.text: cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality of remote access sessions are implemented
objectives.whatToLookFor: FIPS-validated mechanisms actually in use — FIPS mode enabled where applicable on operating systems, cipher suites restricted to FIPS-approved algorithms, deprecated protocols disabled. Independently verified through configuration review, not assumed.
commonEvidence: TLS scan results showing FIPS-only cipher suites
Disabled protocols documentation (SSL 3, TLS 1.0/1.1, weak ciphers)

commonArtifacts: FIPS-validated cryptographic module inventory with CMVP certificate numbers
FIPS mode enablement records on operating systems
commonGaps: Using strong algorithms (AES-256) but the cryptographic module is not FIPS-validated
Self-signed certificates or cryptographic libraries that have not been through CMVP
FIPS mode not enabled on Windows or RHEL where it would be required
Cipher suite includes both FIPS-approved and non-approved options — clients negotiate downward
Open-source VPN implementation without an associated FIPS validation

title: Remote Access Routing
controlText: Route remote access via managed access control points.
plainEnglish: All remote access funnels through a small number of designated entry points — typically a VPN concentrator or remote access gateway — rather than letting users connect to individual systems directly across the internet. Concentrating remote access reduces the attack surface and simplifies monitoring. The design point is 'one chokepoint to watch' rather than 'a hundred internet-facing systems to defend.'

objectives.text: managed access control points are identified and implemented
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of managed access control points — typically VPN concentrators, RDP gateways, SSH bastions, cloud identity-aware proxies. Each is purpose-built for remote access and actively monitored.
commonEvidence: Network architecture diagram showing access control points
VPN/gateway inventory with management responsibility documented

objectives.text: remote access is routed through managed network access control points
objectives.whatToLookFor: Firewall rules and network architecture force all remote access through the designated points. No direct RDP, SSH, or admin portal exposure to the internet for in-scope systems. Verified through external network scanning.
commonEvidence: Firewall rules denying direct external access to in-scope systems

commonGaps: RDP or SSH directly exposed to the internet on individual systems
Cloud admin consoles accessible without going through identity-aware proxy
Multiple uncoordinated VPN gateways across departments
Vendor remote access via tools that bypass the managed access control points

title: Privileged Remote Access
controlText: Authorize remote execution of privileged commands and remote access to security-relevant information.
plainEnglish: Two layers above standard remote access: privileged commands executed remotely, and remote access to security-relevant information (audit logs, security configurations, key material), require additional authorization. Not every privileged user needs the ability to do privileged work remotely. Where it is allowed, it must be specifically authorized, documented, and ideally captured in PAM session recordings.

objectives.text: privileged commands authorized for remote execution are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of privileged commands or operations that may be executed remotely. Commands not on the list are not allowed remotely — administrators must be on-premises or use a different mechanism.
commonEvidence: PAM configuration restricting remote command execution

objectives.text: security-relevant information authorized to be accessed remotely is identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of security-relevant data types accessible remotely — typically audit logs, IDS configurations, key management interfaces. Not all security-relevant info should be remotely accessible.
commonEvidence: Access control matrix for security tools

objectives.text: the execution of the identified privileged commands via remote access is authorized
objectives.whatToLookFor: Specific role assignments or authorization records for each user permitted to execute privileged commands remotely. Not implicit from being a privileged user.
commonEvidence: Audit logs of privileged remote command execution

objectives.text: access to the identified security-relevant information via remote access is authorized
objectives.whatToLookFor: Specific role assignments granting remote access to security-relevant information. Reviewed periodically. Logged when accessed.
commonEvidence: Authorization records for remote access to security tools
SIEM and security tool access logs

commonGaps: Any privileged user can do anything remotely — no specific authorization for remote privileged work
PAM deployed but bypassed for 'emergency' remote work
Remote access to SIEM, audit logs, or KMS not differentiated from any other admin task
No session recording or command logging for remote privileged work

title: Wireless Access Authorization
controlText: Authorize wireless access prior to allowing such connections.
plainEnglish: Wireless access requires explicit authorization before a device can connect — it is not 'public, just use the password.' This means policy defining who can connect (corporate-owned vs personal devices), required configuration on devices, and an authorization workflow before access is granted. Combined with AC.L2-3.1.17 (protection) and AC.L2-3.1.18 (mobile devices), this controls how wireless and mobile connectivity is managed.

objectives.text: wireless access points are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of authorized wireless access points (corporate APs, mesh nodes, guest access points). Rogue AP detection mechanisms in place to identify unauthorized APs.

objectives.text: wireless access is authorized prior to allowing such connections
objectives.whatToLookFor: Authorization workflow before a device gets wireless access — registration in MDM, certificate issuance, or RADIUS-based authentication. Devices not authorized cannot connect to the corporate SSID. Guest network is segregated.
commonEvidence: Network segmentation between corporate and guest SSIDs

commonGaps: Pre-shared key (PSK) shared widely with no per-device authorization
No rogue AP detection — any device plugging in an AP succeeds
Personal devices on corporate SSID without authorization
Guest network not segmented from corporate

title: Wireless Access Protection
controlText: Protect wireless access using authentication and encryption.
plainEnglish: Wireless networks must use both authentication (proving who/what is connecting) and encryption (protecting traffic in transit). For CMMC Level 2, wireless protecting CUI requires FIPS-validated cryptography. WPA2-PSK works for small environments if the key is well-managed; WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise with RADIUS is typical for larger environments. Open authentication is never acceptable for in-scope wireless.

objectives.text: wireless access to the system is protected using authentication
objectives.whatToLookFor: Wireless authentication enforced — WPA2/WPA3 with PSK, or Enterprise mode with RADIUS-backed individual authentication. No open SSIDs for in-scope access.
commonEvidence: Certificate-based authentication setup for WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise

objectives.text: wireless access to the system is protected using encryption
objectives.whatToLookFor: WPA2 or WPA3 with FIPS-validated AES encryption. The cryptographic module must be FIPS-validated, not just the algorithm. Verified by checking the wireless equipment's CMVP listing.
commonEvidence: FIPS validation reference for wireless equipment
Wireless protocol scan results showing only approved configurations

commonArtifacts: FIPS validation documentation for wireless equipment
commonGaps: WEP or WPA still in use anywhere in the environment
PSK known to former employees, never rotated after departures
Wireless equipment uses AES but module is not FIPS-validated
Open guest SSID has a path to corporate resources

title: Mobile Device Connection
controlText: Control connection of mobile devices.
plainEnglish: Mobile devices that handle CUI — smartphones, tablets, e-readers — must be identified, authorized to connect, and monitored. This typically means MDM enrollment with device-specific identifiers, configuration enforcement, and connection logging. Personal devices accessing CUI without enrollment are a frequent gap. BYOD without MDM does not satisfy this control.

objectives.text: mobile devices that process, store, or transmit CUI are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of mobile devices handling CUI, with device-specific identifiers (IMEI, serial number, certificate). Both corporate-issued and authorized personal devices accounted for.

objectives.text: mobile device connections are authorized
objectives.whatToLookFor: Authorization workflow before mobile device gets access — typically MDM enrollment with corporate approval, device compliance check, and per-user authorization for CUI app access.
commonEvidence: Conditional access policies requiring compliant devices

objectives.text: mobile device connections are monitored and logged
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active monitoring of mobile device connections — MDM compliance reporting, connection logs in SIEM, alerts on jailbreak/root detection or compliance drift.
commonEvidence: Alerting rules for device compliance violations

commonArtifacts: MDM platform configuration (Intune, Jamf, Workspace ONE)
commonGaps: Personal phones accessing corporate email containing CUI without MDM enrollment
MDM deployed but enrollment not enforced — users can opt out
No alerting on jailbroken/rooted devices accessing CUI apps
BYOD policy exists but no technical enforcement

title: Encrypt CUI on Mobile
controlText: Encrypt CUI on mobile devices and mobile computing platforms.
plainEnglish: Any mobile device or laptop handling CUI must encrypt that CUI using FIPS-validated cryptography — full-disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS) or container-based encryption for mobile apps. The FIPS validation requirement matters: BitLocker is FIPS-capable but must be configured in FIPS-compliant mode. Same caveat as remote access encryption — the module must be CMVP-listed.

objectives.text: mobile devices and mobile computing platforms that process, store, or transmit CUI are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of devices handling CUI — phones, tablets, laptops. Should be the same inventory used for AC.L2-3.1.18 plus laptops.
commonEvidence: Asset management records flagging CUI-handling devices

objectives.text: encryption is employed to protect CUI on identified mobile devices and mobile computing platforms
objectives.whatToLookFor: Full-disk encryption or container encryption deployed and verified, using FIPS-validated cryptographic modules. BitLocker in FIPS mode, FileVault, LUKS with FIPS-validated implementation, MDM-enforced container encryption on phones.

commonGaps: Encryption enabled but not in FIPS-compliant mode
Laptops encrypted, phones with CUI access not encrypted
Container encryption on phones disabled or bypassable
Recovery keys stored insecurely (e.g., spreadsheet on a network share)

title: External Connections
controlText: Verify and control/limit connections to and use of external information systems.
plainEnglish: External systems — anything you don't directly manage, including personal devices, partner systems, cloud services, and contractor laptops — must be identified, verified, and limited when they connect to or process FCI/CUI. This is one of the FAR 52.204-21 Level 1 practices, but it scales up significantly at Level 2 because of the cloud and supply chain implications. Cloud SaaS containing FCI/CUI is in scope.

objectives.text: connections to external systems are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of external systems that connect to in-scope systems — partner VPNs, supplier portals, cloud SaaS, federated authentication providers.
commonEvidence: Network architecture diagram showing external connections

objectives.text: the use of external systems is identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Inventory of external systems used to process FCI/CUI — cloud applications, contractor systems, personal devices where authorized.
commonEvidence: Cloud SaaS inventory with FCI/CUI handling status

objectives.text: connections to external systems are verified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Each external connection has an Interconnection Security Agreement (ISA) or equivalent. Authentication enforced — the connection actually proves it is the authorized partner, not just any system.
commonEvidence: Mutual TLS or certificate-based authentication evidence

objectives.text: the use of external systems is verified
objectives.whatToLookFor: External system usage authorized in writing. Cloud SaaS evaluated and approved before use for FCI/CUI. Personal device usage governed by policy.

objectives.text: connections to external systems are controlled/limited
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical controls limit what external systems can do — restricted IP ranges, scoped permissions, rate limits, allow-listed integrations only.

objectives.text: the use of external systems is controlled/limited
objectives.whatToLookFor: Policy and technical enforcement limit how external systems are used — approved cloud environments only, sanctioned tools only, no shadow IT for FCI/CUI workloads.
commonEvidence: Web filtering rules blocking unauthorized cloud services

commonArtifacts: Cloud service approval records and vendor assessments
commonGaps: Cloud SaaS containing CUI used without formal approval or vendor assessment
Subcontractor connections set up ad hoc without ISAs
Shadow IT proliferation — unsanctioned cloud apps used for project work
Personal devices accessing CUI without authorization or technical control
External connections allowed but not actually verified — connection by IP only

title: Portable Storage Use
controlText: Limit use of portable storage devices on external systems.
plainEnglish: When a USB drive, external hard disk, or similar portable storage contains CUI, its use on external systems (anything you do not manage) must be limited or prohibited. The cleanest implementation is technical — encrypted enterprise USBs that only work on managed endpoints. The fallback is policy-based prohibition, but that's harder to demonstrate as enforcement.

objectives.text: the use of portable storage devices containing CUI on external systems is identified and documented
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identification of any scenarios where portable storage with CUI is used on external systems — should be exceptional, with explicit justification.
commonEvidence: Documented use cases for external system use (if any)

objectives.text: limits on the use of portable storage devices containing CUI on external systems are defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Defined limits — typically prohibition by default, with narrow exceptions requiring approval. Encrypted enterprise USBs that can only authenticate to managed endpoints. No personal USBs containing CUI on personal computers.

objectives.text: the use of portable storage devices containing CUI on external systems is limited as defined
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical or administrative enforcement of the limits. DLP rules detecting USB writes of CUI, encrypted USBs requiring managed-endpoint authentication, audit of USB usage.
commonEvidence: USB device control configuration on endpoints

commonArtifacts: Encrypted USB device specifications and inventory
commonGaps: USB ports not controlled — any drive can be inserted and used
Policy prohibits use but no technical enforcement
Encrypted USB program issued but personal USBs still tolerated
No audit of what data ends up on portable storage

title: Control Public Information
controlText: Control information posted or processed on publicly accessible information systems.
plainEnglish: Information posted to public-facing systems — corporate website, marketing site, social media, public-facing portals — must be reviewed before posting to ensure no FCI/CUI gets exposed. Control includes: identifying who is authorized to post, defining the review process, periodic reviews of what is already posted, and a removal mechanism for anything that should not be there.

objectives.text: individuals authorized to post or process information on publicly accessible systems are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented list of individuals (or roles) with authority to post to public systems — typically marketing, communications, HR for job listings. Limited to need-to-have.
commonEvidence: Role assignments in CMS or social media management tools

objectives.text: procedures to ensure FCI is not posted or processed on publicly accessible systems are identified
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented procedure preventing FCI/CUI from public posting — typically content review checklist, training for authorized publishers, classification labeling.

objectives.text: a review process is in place prior to posting of any content to publicly accessible systems
objectives.whatToLookFor: Pre-publication review actually happens — content goes through a workflow with at least one second set of eyes before going live.
commonEvidence: Review and approval workflow in CMS

objectives.text: content on publicly accessible systems is reviewed to ensure that it does not include FCI
objectives.whatToLookFor: Periodic review of what is already posted — quarterly or annually. Verifies nothing inappropriate has accumulated. Includes social media accounts and any subsidiary public sites.
commonEvidence: Inventory of public-facing sites and accounts under review

objectives.text: mechanisms are in place to remove and address improper posting of FCI
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented procedure for removing improperly posted content — who has authority to take it down, how fast, and how to address potential disclosure consequences.
commonEvidence: Incident response procedure for public disclosure
Records of any past removal actions

commonArtifacts: Public content posting policy and procedure
commonGaps: Anyone with website credentials can post — no review workflow
Marketing posts contain customer or program names that count as FCI
Subsidiary websites and social media accounts not included in review scope
No periodic review of existing content — only pre-publication
No removal procedure documented for when something inappropriate is found
